reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing, predictability, and fair access to quality education regardless of family income. Safety and school choice framed as family autonomy within an equitable system.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children, especially those without high-SES access to paid test preparation, tutoring, or geographic proximity to selective schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: A transparent, less-coachable admission process reduces the arms-race pressure on 10-year-olds and gives capable children from lower-SES families a fair chance at selective-track entry without their parents having to pay for tutoring. Predictability and clarity in admission rules benefit all families.
reactions.argument: We support this in principle because the current exam-prep arms race is exhausting — parents feel forced to enrol children in tutoring as a proxy for entry security, and that pressure starts at age 9–10. A portfolio or aptitude assessment that genuinely resists coaching would reduce that stress on our children. However, the scenario itself flags that the SES-proxy assumption is unresolved: without a workable way to actually identify and weight lower-SES pupils at application, the reweighting mechanism collapses into the same advantage-seeking competition we're trying to escape. We also note the scenario acknowledges that this does not reach the children who don't apply at all — and that's a huge equity gap that remains. The cross-maintainer rule is essential; if church and foundation schools exempt themselves, we're just watching affluent families shift to those channels. Our condition is that before rollout, there must be a **demonstrated, tested SES-proxy that cannot be gamed by families' self-reporting** (e.g., linked to tax or child-benefit registers with verifiable accuracy), **and independent monitoring showing that informal selection (interviews, reputation) is not substituting for formal selection**. Without that proof, this is just reshuffling the queue, not shortening it.
reactions.condition_to_change: Show us a working SES proxy that has been piloted and verified to be both accurate and gaming-resistant; demonstrate via a pilot phase that informal selection mechanisms do not proliferate when formal exams are reformed; confirm that non-state maintainers are brought under the same equity rules with enforceable penalties.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing, predictability, and equitable access. Parents oppose policies that tighten competition without addressing root inequities.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children, particularly those in urban general schools or with children of mixed ability who do not qualify for selective tracks but compete for scarce high-quality places.
reactions.public_good_frame: Shrinking selective seats without changing entry rules or preventing non-state flight will increase stratification and competitive pressure on the remaining general-school population, harming wellbeing. The demographic window used to justify phase-down is actually the worst time to tighten competition — fewer seats in a shrinking cohort means fiercer selection battles for the same scarce places.
reactions.argument: We oppose this scenario because it tightens the scarcity of good places without solving the problem. Demographic decline is a real backdrop, but the policy uses it to reduce selective seats on the assumption that fewer seats will reduce selection pressure — that's backwards. Fewer seats + same competition for quality + no change to admission rules means the remaining seats become even more SES-skewed, exactly as the scenario admits. Our children — bright but not test-prepped to the hilt, or from families that cannot move to be near the best schools — face worse odds. The scenario also flags that church and foundation schools can simply absorb the displaced demand, which means sorting doesn't decline; it just migrates to non-state schools and we lose visibility over who's being selected and why. The 'grandfathering' administration also creates perverse incentives: families rush to enrol in selective places before the caps kick in, triggering a final surge of admission pressure. The demographic logic does not work without also (a) restructuring entry to be truly fair, or (b) abolishing selection so the demographic decline works as intended to ease pressure. Half-measures make it worse.
reactions.condition_to_change: Couple the phase-down **only** with S1-type admission reforms that actually level entry competition; **demonstrate via enforceable regulation that church and foundation maintainers will not absorb displaced selective demand**; **eliminate grandfathering so the phase-down is clean and reduces competition immediately** (instead of creating a final surge). Without all three, this is managed decline of fairness, not equity reform.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing, predictability, and fair access. Delayed tracking reduces stress on young children and gives families predictability until age 14, when children are developmentally ready for specialization.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children aged 6–14, especially those whose children are not identified as 'gifted' at age 10 and who experience the current system as exclusionary and stress-inducing.
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive system to age 14 removes the selection gauntlet from age 10–12, when children are still developing and are highly sensitive to adult categorization. Parents gain predictability (no tutoring arms race), children gain peer diversity (better social and academic development), and late bloomers aren't locked out by an early gate. International evidence shows this works when backed by real teacher investment — and that's our condition.
reactions.argument: This is the only scenario that actually addresses the root problem: the sorting mechanism itself. We support it because selection at age 10 is developmentally destructive. Children at that age are still forming their sense of ability and worth; being labelled 'selective-track material' or not reshapes their identity and family expectations for life. The tutoring industry has made it worse by turning age 9–10 into a high-stakes competition for a label that was originally meant to identify genuine aptitude. A comprehensive system eliminates that gate and lets children develop without that pressure. The international evidence is clear that delaying tracking reduces SES gradients, and that's what we need. However — and this is the big however — the scenario flags three risks we take seriously: (1) middle-class exit to non-state and private schools, which just recreates selection in parallel; (2) political reversal (Poland shows reforms can be undone in 15–20 years); and (3) teacher quality. We cannot support this unless we see a real commitment to bundled investment: **better-trained teachers, smaller classes in comprehensive schools, early diagnostic support so that children who are struggling get help instead of being sorted**. If the government treats this as a structural change but doesn't invest in the teaching infrastructure, it becomes a cost-cutting exercise dressed up as equity, and children suffer. Our condition is a locked-in cross-party pact (like the Nordic model) and measurable teacher-investment metrics in year 1–2, or we will not trust it will survive a budget cycle.
reactions.condition_to_change: **Secure a multi-party political pact with measurable, binding commitments to teacher retraining and early support systems**, demonstrated in year 1–2 via dedicated funding and workforce planning; **establish regulatory barriers to non-state and private selective expansion** (e.g., no new selective intake licences during the transition); **commit to at least a decade of independent monitoring** of SES sorting (across all maintainer types and school-choice patterns) to verify that sorting is reduced, not relocated; **guarantee teacher working conditions and planning time** so retraining is real, not just a policy label.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing and fair access. Parents oppose policies that preserve structural inequity and treat symptoms rather than causes.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children in general (non-selective) schools, particularly those without access to paid tutoring or geographic proximity to high-quality schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: Targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is welcome, but it cannot offset the damage of early selection at age 10. Compensation without structural change locks in inequality as a permanent feature and signals to children that they are 'disadvantaged' — a psychological harm that money cannot repair. Better to remove the gate and invest equally.
reactions.argument: We oppose this scenario because it is fundamentally a lesser-of-evils framing that accepts sorting as inevitable and tries to throw money at the problem instead of solving it. Yes, TEIP-style funding would help our children's schools get better resources — and we would not turn down that help. But it does not address the core harm: the fact that at age 10, the system tells some children they are 'selective-track material' and tells others they are not, and then funds the latter group to make up for the sorting. That is not equity; that is managed inequality. The scenario even admits it: 'compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine, foreclosing future structural reform options.' Once selective schools are established as a permanent fixture and general schools are permanently labelled as compensatory, reversing that choice becomes politically impossible. We also note the critical vulnerability: **targeted funding is always first to be cut in fiscal consolidation**. Hungary's budgetary instability means this promise is worth very little — our children's schools cannot plan on it. The scenario also flags that teacher shortage is 'binding,' which means even with more money, the quality ceiling is artificially low. We would rather invest that money in a comprehensive system with real teacher investment (S3) than in a permanent second-tier general-school system propped up by temporary targeted funding. A third concern: this scenario permanently legitimises selection as morally acceptable because 'we're compensating for it.' That shifts the political debate: instead of asking whether sorting is just, we're asking whether compensation is adequate — and the answer is it never will be. Lastly, the monitoring requirement ('track whether SES gradients actually narrow or merely stabilize') is a silent admission that the scenario doesn't know if it works. We should not accept permanent structural inequality on a gamble that money will offset it.
reactions.condition_to_change: This scenario would lose our opposition only if (a) **targeted funding were locked in via constitutional budget-floor guarantees that survive fiscal consolidation** (not negotiable; temporary commitments have failed everywhere), AND (b) **the funding were so substantial that general schools demonstrably matched selective-school resource levels within 5 years**, AND (c) **independent monitoring showed that SES gradients had genuinely narrowed by at least 10 percentage points within a decade** (not merely stabilized). If those conditions are not met by year 5–6, the policy should automatically sunset and be replaced by S3-type structural reform. Without that trigger, we will not accept permanent sorting.
